content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Uncertainty Prompting: Enhancing LLM Confidence Calibration via Contrastive Queries",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often display overconfidence and poor calibration in their predictions, particularly on ambiguous or out-of-distribution queries. Existing prompting techniques inadequately elicit well-calibrated uncertainty estimates from LLMs.",
  "motivation": "Reliable uncertainty quantification is critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains such as healthcare and law. As LLMs are increasingly used as decision-support tools, improving their ability to express calibrated confidence is essential to prevent harmful outcomes.",
  "method": "We propose a counterfactual uncertainty prompting (CUP) technique that augments standard prompts with contrastive counterfactual versions of the input (e.g., minimally perturbed negatives or semantically altered paraphrases). The LLM is asked to answer both the original and counterfactual prompts, explicitly providing its confidence or probability in each case. By comparing the spread and consistency of confidence across these paired prompts, we extract a more robust uncertainty estimate. The prompt template encourages the model to justify differences in its confidence, further exposing underlying uncertainty.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CUP on question answering and factual recall datasets (e.g., TriviaQA, TruthfulQA) and assess calibration using Brier score and Expected Calibration Error. Baselines include vanilla prompting, chain-of-thought, and self-consistency. Human annotation of ambiguous cases will supplement quantitative metrics to assess practical alignment with uncertainty."
}
